perturbation.original_text: AJR shall indemnify each XC Indemnified Party and hold each XC Indemnified Party harmless from and against any and all Losses incurred by any XC Indemnified Party, directly or indirectly, as a result of or based upon any of the following: (i) any breach by AJR of any of its agreements, covenants or obligations hereunder; or (ii) the use of any of the Licensed Materials in a manner that is not permitted hereby.
perturbation.changed_text: AJR shall indemnify each XC Indemnified Party and hold each XC Indemnified Party harmless from and against any and all Losses incurred by any XC Indemnified Party, directly or indirectly, as a result of or based upon any of the following: (i) any breach by AJR of any of its agreements, covenants or obligations hereunder; or (ii) the use of any of the Licensed Materials in a manner that is not permitted hereby, unless the damages were accidental.
perturbation.explanation: Adding an exception for accidental damages to the indemnification clause contradicts the principle of strict liability for breaches of contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA)
perturbation.law_explanation: 42 U.S. Code § 9607 imposes strict liability on certain parties. By adding 'unless the damages were accidental,' this limits the scope of indemnification, potentially conflicting with the strict liability provisions under CERCLA, where liability can arise regardless of intent or negligence.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception ('unless the damages were accidental') to the indemnification clause, which directly contradicts the principle of strict liability under CERCLA, as explained in the Law Explanation. 42 U.S. Code § 9607, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, imposes liability 'Notwithstanding any other provision or rule of law'. By exempting accidental damages, the changed text attempts to circumvent this strict liability, creating a legal contradiction as it undermines the law's intent to hold parties responsible regardless of intent or negligence. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where the change makes the contractual terms unenforceable by creating an exception to strict liability.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, upon the occurrence of an AJR Event of Default any of the Warrants which have not been exercised shall automatically terminate and be void and all of the Shares and any shares of Common Stock issued in the exercise of the Warrants shall be purchasable by -9- XC at the purchase price of $1.00. Upon XC tendering the purchase price, AJR shall promptly deliver to XC the certificates for the Shares and any shares of Common Stock issued upon the exercise of the Warrants, together with duly executed blank stock powers.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, upon the occurrence of an AJR Event of Default any of the Warrants which have not been exercised shall automatically terminate and be void and all of the Shares and any shares of Common Stock issued in the exercise of the Warrants shall be purchasable by -9- XC at the purchase price of $0.00. Upon XC tendering the purchase price, AJR shall promptly deliver to XC the certificates for the Shares and any shares of Common Stock issued upon the exercise of the Warrants, together with duly executed blank stock powers.
perturbation.explanation: Setting the repurchase price to $0.00 contradicts the requirements for fair compensation.
perturbation.law_explanation: 8 Del. C. § 160 imposes restrictions on a corporation's repurchase of its own stock. Setting the repurchase price to $0.00 could be construed as a violation of fiduciary duty and fairness, potentially conflicting with Delaware law principles regarding fair value in corporate transactions. Requiring AJR to hand over the share at $0.00 effectively cancels the previous deal between the two entities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 211 Â§ 212 Â§ 213 Â§ 214 Â§ 215 Â§ 216 Â§ 217 Â§ 218 Â§ 219 Â§ 220 Â§ 221 Â§ 222 Â§ 223 Â§ 224 Â§ 225 Â§ 226 Â§ 227 Â§ 228 Â§ 229 Â§ 230 Â§ 231 Â§ 232 Â§ 233 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter VII. Meetings, Elections, Voting and Notice Â§ 211. Meetings of stockholders. (a) (1) Meetings of stockholders may be held at such place, either within or without this State as may be designated by or in the manner provided in the certificate of incorporation or bylaws, or if not so designated, as determined by the board of directors. If, pursuant to this paragraph or the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws of the corporation, the board of directors is authorized to determine the place of a meeting of stockholders, the board of directors may, in its sole discretion, determine that the meeting shall not be held at any place, but may instead be held solely by means of remote communication as authorized by paragraph (a)(2) of this section. (2) If authorized by the board of directors in its sole discretion, and subject to such guidelines and procedures as the board of directors may adopt, stockholders and proxyholders not physically present at a meeting of stockholders may, by means of remote communication: a. Participate in a meeting of stockholders; and b. Be deemed present in person and vote at a meeting of stockholders, whether such meeting is to be held at a designated place or solely by means of remote communication, provided that (i) the corporation shall implement reasonable measures to verify that each person deemed present and permitted to vote at the meeting by means of remote communication is a stockholder or proxyholder, (ii) the corporation shall implement reasonable measures to provide such stockholders and proxyholders a reasonable opportunity to participate in the meeting and to vote on matters submitted to the stockholders, including an opportunity to read or hear the proceedings of the meet
perturbation.justification: The changed text sets the repurchase price at $0.00, which is a significant deviation from fair compensation. The Law Explanation states that this could be construed as a violation of fiduciary duty and fairness under Delaware law principles. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on meetings of stockholders and doesn't directly address stock repurchase prices, the Law Explanation's reference to Delaware law (8 Del. C. § 160) and the general principles of fairness in corporate transactions are enough to determine that setting a repurchase price to $0.00 contradicts these principles. The changed text effectively nullifies the previous agreement regarding the value of the shares, creating a contradiction.
